name: credit_union_voicebot_scam_blocking
premise: Fraud manager DeShawn Miller must decide whether to deploy a voicebot that blocks outbound transfers when it detects probable 'grandparent scam' coaching on a call. Privacy counsel Anya Petrova warns the system will record and analyze intimate conversations and may wrongly block bilingual elders who use code-switching. If DeShawn deploys it, losses may drop and vulnerable members may be protected. If it misfires, members may miss rent or medical payments and lose trust in the credit union. A surge of scams is happening now and the board wants action before the next statement cycle.

agents.name: DeShawn Miller
agents.identity: I am the fraud manager responsible for protecting members from scams while ensuring payment access is reliable and not biased by speech patterns.
agents.goal: I want to stop scam losses without turning banking into a monitored phone booth that blocks legitimate urgent transfers.

agents.name: Anya Petrova
agents.identity: I am the privacy counsel responsible for limiting surveillance and ensuring fraud tools do not discriminate or create covert recordings without consent.
agents.goal: I want deployment limited to opt-in warnings and post-transaction review, because analyzing calls to block payments is too intrusive and too error-prone.

choices: I will deploy the voicebot as a blocking control with a rapid human override line, accepting privacy intrusion to prevent ongoing scam harm.
I will deploy only non-blocking warnings and enhanced verification, accepting higher scam losses to avoid intrusive call analysis and wrongful blocks.
forced_choice.call_to_action: Scam losses are spiking and members are calling daily in distress. DeShawn Miller must choose between a blocking voicebot with overrides or non-blocking warnings only. What does DeShawn Miller do?
